rationale: The citing paper explicitly frames its contribution against prior “tariff-choice biases” and studies plan-switching inertia/salience in plan recommendations, while the cited paper is directly about tariff choice under consumer learning with switching costs. This strong construct overlap (tariff/plan choice, switching frictions, retention behavior) makes the cited work a plausible theory/mechanism source rather than an incidental mention, even though the raw evidence is only a DOI-level match.
